heading: Monitoring Data Storage Granularity
body:
Atlas stores metrics data at various granularity levels. For each
granularity level, Atlas computes metrics as averages of the
reported metrics at the next finer granularity level.

> **Example:** At the end of a 60-minute period, Atlas generates a 1-hour
> metrics report. Atlas computes the value of the Connections
> metric in the 1-hour report as the average of all
> values for the Connections metric across the 60 1-minute reports
> that it generates in that hour.

Atlas gathers metrics data at a 1-minute granularity unless you qualify for
premium monitoring. However, Atlas gathers **Replication Lag**
and **Replication Headroom** metrics at an 85-second granularity.

When metrics data reach the end of their retention period, Atlas
compacts them into a single unit of the next broader granularity level.

> **Example:** After 48 hours' worth of data is collected, Atlas compacts each
> group of 60 minutes into a single unit of an hour. After 63 days,
> Atlas compacts each group of 24 hours into a single unit of a day.

> **Note:** When you scale a cluster to M40 tier or higher, the cluster
> qualifies for premium monitoring. To view historical metrics data for your
> scaled cluster, you must adjust the granularity to one-minute intervals.
> This is because premium monitoring displays 10-second metrics granularity while
> basic monitoring displays 1-minute granularity.
